speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is February 23rd, 1996. I'm interviewing George Kohn, K-O-H-N. My name is Cookie Schwartz. We are in the city of Deerfield, Florida, United States of America, and the language is English. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I'm Cookie Schwartz, and today I'm interviewing George Kohn. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Show us or tell us when it's two minutes to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name and your spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is George Kohn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: G-E-O-R-G-E K-O-H-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name at birth? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: March the 7th, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth, your birth name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birth name, Cmielow, C-M-I-E-L-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your birth city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The neighborhood where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you were born. And your country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth name? Your exact name as--

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth name was Josef.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My Jewish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Josef? J-- J-O-S-- J-O-S-E-F.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the way-- that's the way I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, George, tell me about your life before the war, your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1926, I was 6 years old, that's when I started public school. And afternoon-- afternoons I went to cheder, like a Jewish school. And I would spend most of the-- most of the afternoons until 8:00 in the evening. In other words, I had a full day of schools. And life was going on. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we had-- had a wonderful family. We were a family of nine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were five brothers and four sisters. And my grandparents lived right beside us. And the-- the town was a small town of 125 Jewish families there. And-- and my father was a very successful businessman. He was in the shoe business, manufacturing and retail. And he was a very Orthodox Jew. He went every day to shul. And for the holidays, he used to bring home people, poor people for-- for meals. And so it was-- we were-- it was a wonderful life, you know. After, in 1934, I decided to go-- to go away from home at the age of 13 or 14. I don't remember. So I decided to go to yeshiva high school, like, that was after public school. So I packed a knapsack, and my father gave me one-way ticket. And he said, go. If you want to go, go. So I went. So I ended up in the city. That's where the yeshiva was that was recommended by-- by some rabbis, a city rabbi. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the distance from-- from our city was 300 miles away from home. When I got there, naturally, I was young, I missed my friends. And I decided after two weeks I want to go home. I wasn't too happy. But my father wrote me a letter. Stay there. Stick it out. You'll make it. So I did. But accommodation was-- you know, people, I come in, I'd ask for sleeping accommodation. And I used to eat every day in a different-- in a different house, you know. They used to support the yeshiva. We were maybe 200 boys in all that yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of the yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the city was Kinsk, Kinsk. Konskie in Polish [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know how to spell it. Yeah, I could. I could spell it. It doesn't make any difference. And I spend there a whole season. I couldn't wait for the [? holidays. ?] That was right after the Jewish holidays, and I couldn't wait already to get home for the-- for the holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandfather was very happy that I went to yeshiva. And I mean, I couldn't wait to show him what I accomplished in six months, you know? So I came home for Passover, you know, and I spent the holiday. And who was at home? Your brothers and sisters, can you tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them [INAUDIBLE] were. Well, before that, that's a-- that's a story. Before we-- before I went to yeshiva, so we had my older brother, Hymie. He left in 1928. He left home for Montreal, Canada. And a year-- a year-- a year after, 1929, my younger brother, his younger brother-- he was my older brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He followed to-- to Montreal. He left for Montreal, the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Murray.

speaker: interviewer
text: Murray.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] He was-- was the younger brother, followed Hymie. They were together. And right after that, in-- in 1937-- oh, no, 1935, my next brother-- there were three of them-- the third brother left for Israel. They didn't want to stay in the whole small town, not because they didn't have what to eat or they-- they were short of anything, but they wanted to travel. They wanted to get away from-- from Poland. So he ended up in Tel Aviv, you know. And they struggled in those years. It was very hard times in Israel. We used to send him parcels and all kind of things. And in a year, a few years later, in 1937, my older sister, Ray, she left to Montreal to-- to join-- to marry one of my brother's friends, Nathan Heisler. And she got married in '37 when she got to Montreal. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in '39, in 1939, just before the war, so my older brother came for a visit home. It was a great joy to have him there. You know, it was very exciting. And unfortunately, he had to leave just before the war to run away from the-- there were rumors the-- the war is going-- is going to come very quickly, and so he left. He went. He managed to get back to-- to New York. He got the last boat went out-- out of Paris. At that time, as a matter of fact, they sank-- the German-- the war got on and they sank a boat. We thought for sure he was on the boat. Well, anyway, that's not the story. But and then I went back to yeshiva, in 19-- [INAUDIBLE] 1930-- after Pesach, it was still '34. And I went for another season. And that summer, I remember that summer, after Pesach, I had my younger brother-- oh, I didn't tell you the rest of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we have a sister, we had a sister, my older sister was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's her name? Matla. She was-- she was-- she was born 1917. That was my next sister. And after that, we had another-- after-- after-- then, I was next. I was born next. And next was my other sister, Bayla. She was born in 1923, something like that. After her was my younger brother, which he survived the war, Martin. He was born in 1925. And then we had a-- a younger sister was born in 1929. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1929. Her name, Toba. She was the youngest. So we were-- that was the-- that was the left of the family, you know? We were-- we remained at home, you know? And we had a wonderful life and everything was going nicely. And we-- and we weren't-- we weren't lacking anything. My father made a good living. And unfortunately, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother. How she--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, yeah. My mother-- my mother was a-- was a business lady. She was-- she never stayed home. Like the older-- the older sisters took care of the domestic problems. And the business and the home all was together, you know? Like in the back we had a home and the business, the store, was in the front. So she was the business lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father worked in the manufacturing, which we used-- used to produce 150 pair of shoes a week outside, like home workers. They were all made by hand, you know? They were very popular. And we made-- we had all our customers-- we had-- oh, by the way, we had this factory in our hometown which produced china. And they employed 2,500 people there, Polish, most of them Polish. Most of those people were-- were our customers for shoes, you know? Therefore, we needed-- you need a lot of shoes. And it was wonderful. And-- Did you experience any anti-Semitism in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We had-- we had a lot of problems with-- always-- there were always problems with the-- with the Gentile, you know? They were always chasing. But-- but my father was very nice with most of our customers. They liked him very much. And they didn't show much hate to us, you know, because they needed us. We needed them. And everything was going very nicely. And it was a wonderful experience, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: After the yeshiva, you came home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, after-- no, after yeshiva-- that's where I'm getting, because I made notes on it, you know? After the yeshiva-- so I went for another season. And after that-- oh, while I was there, that-- that season after-- after Passover, I went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I brought down my kid brother. I got him into a special teacher, you know, for the summer, you know, with room and board, you know, which cost a lot of money. And he was the only student for this private teacher who was teaching him, like, Hebrew and all the-- from the Bible. But he stayed only one summer and then went back. He didn't want to come back. So I went back, but I went to a different-- to a higher-- higher grade yeshiva. It was a different city, by the name [INAUDIBLE]. It was maybe 50 miles away from the first city where I went. There, I spent the rest of my yeshiva study, until 1937. In '37, so I came home. And my father decided you're not going back. We need you for the business. You're a big boy. You better help in the business, because most of our family were gone, you know? The rest was younger kids and I was the oldest practically. So I helped out my older sister, which was a few years older than me, but she used to-- she had to be in the house for housework. So I was the chief next to my father in-- in the business. So it was wonderful. I enjoyed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in between business time-- so we had a rabbi in-- in our town, you know, which was very popular, well known all over. They used to come to visit him from all over, from Warsaw, from Lodz, for the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a rabbi and I study.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I kept study-- Rabbi Epstein, you know, Alter Epstein. He was a well-known rabbi. And they came from all over to-- like Hasidim, you know? So I used to-- I didn't stay with my father in the congregation. I went to his congregation because there, I was studying, like, the Bible and all that. And it was wonderful. I enjoyed my life. It was very good. And unfortunately, so at-- at the end, my brother left, just before the war, before the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother when he-- when he came for a visit, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother went for-- she went every year. She used to go to a resort, you know, a health resort, like a spa in the mountains. Every year she had to go. So my brother didn't want to let her go herself, so he'll follow with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: There he met a girl. He wasn't married. He met a girl. And he would have gotten married if the war wouldn't broke out, you know? So he left. He left. And it so happened, he-- he left her behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and she as a matter of fact, I found out after the war, she survived the war. Her name was [? Anken. ?] Her family of [? Anken. ?] They were a very prominent family. And I found out from [INAUDIBLE] that she survived the war. She went to Russia, I think. That's where she survived the war. But it happened so that after my brother left-- but the influence, where you know the people from the train, train master. This Mr. [? Vencil ?], would be. He was a good friend of the family. So he got him the last ticket out to go to Warsaw to get the-- the visa to go back to United States. So he was very lucky, but he left everything behind [INAUDIBLE]. He was very lucky he got out of-- alive from that. As a matter of fact, he came with a friend on the-- on the ship when he came. In May, when they arrived to-- to home, when they arrived home, so his friend remained behind. And he was killed by the Nazis after. He couldn't make it back. So he-- he got killed. We found out after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it happened, so 1939, September the 1st, I remember like-- I remember like now it was-- I was-- I went with my friends, you know, out of the city, you know? From [? way over there, ?] we saw planes over-- over our heads, flying. And it looked so funny, you know? We didn't know the war was on. So that-- that was the war, September the 1st.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and we heard bombs drop, bombs that are falling. And the first bomb fell on the station, on the railway-- we had-- we went a rail-- railway station not far. And the bomb hit the-- the gas line, gas pipes, and there was an explosion. And the whole city was smelling gas. We thought for sure they're dropping gas, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was very-- very scary, you know? We got panicky. Everybody got panicky. And-- and that was the war. So it didn't take long, maybe a couple of weeks. There are the Germans. Right away, they came into our city. And immediately, they started to to act up, you know? They start hitting and-- it was-- it was a terrible thing, very frightened. So immediately, they appointed my father for-- to become the head of the Jewish community. So anything they needed, the Germans first away-- first thing to my father. The Germans appointed him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who appointed him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the Germans forced the-- the community to appoint somebody a head, so they-- they picked my father, which he didn't want. He want-- but he had no choice. He had to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they used to come every day to my father to supply 100 boys, young boys, for work for the highways and the mines, whatever they need people to work. So we used-- the quota was 100-- 100 boys every day. So naturally, we were number one on the list, his own boys, that people shouldn't talk that he sent somebody else's boys to work, you know? And that wasn't work. That was torture, you know? They-- they-- they beat us. And I remember my kid brother was at that time, was maybe 12 years old. And I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was 18 or something. So where did you go to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went every day practically, went to work. We came home. You-- still our home was a home. It was nothing. It was only the beginning of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of jobs did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to do on the highways, highways. And also they took us-- sometimes I worked in a mine, you know? They took us down in a shaft to work together with the Polish workers, you know? The-- the mine was producing iron ore, you know, that went to the factories for-- for iron, you know, to-- to produce.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we worked on building barracks, you know, different-- different kind of work. They-- they found work for us. Every day was something else. Mainly we worked on the highways, you know? And-- and that was-- in 19-- and then in 1940 we saw-- notice it everywhere, written on-- on posters that the Russian allow the Jews to come in across the border. Whoever wants to cross the border to Russia is welcome. So I spoke to my father, and I told him it would be a good idea, because this-- this-- we couldn't survive under the circumstances. Let's pack up and let's go. And after all, we'll be safe, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't-- we didn't know what's going to happen. It was only 1940. But my father rejected. He said, no way. We're not going to give up, you know, that wealth we have here with so many shoes stocked up. And by the way, [? he was, ?] everything was hidden, you know, away. Otherwise, the Germans would take it away.

speaker: interviewee
text: He hid a lot of the shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: He hid, yeah. And in the same time, I remember what he did. Same time, he took me down to-- to the cellar, you know, and said, should anything happen, you know, to us-- he-- he knew already there's going to be problem. Said, if anything happened, he took all his possessions, you know, like we had a lot of foreign currency, mostly-- mostly US dollars, you know, that everybody was buying for security.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father, he-- he always bought [INAUDIBLE] from the-- from the people that return from US, they always have US-- US currency-- he bought, and he took like a sugar bowl, porcelain I remember, a sugar bowl. He put everything in that sugar bowl. There were diamonds and-- and gold rings and it was-- and money, you know, a lot of-- I don't remember how many thousands of dollars. Anyway, he put it in there and wrapped it in plastic. He was a very smart man, my father. He was very well-liked in the community. And he took us down to the cellar in the corner and he made a little hole and covered it up with a-- with a rock, you know? Covered it up, nobody would know. He said, remember, this is the spot. Should anything happen to us, this is gonna-- this is gonna make you survive, you know, make you survive. Money will get you anywhere. But don't forget where it-- Well, it was in my mind, you know? So. And it was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he hid all the shoes. He showed me where we-- it was hidden, you know, under the ceiling. And he-- Wherever he found a place, they were hidden. And-- and that was the beginning of the-- it was 1940. And we still went to work. And-- and what happened then, I don't remember. Just a moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you first really felt danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, well, the first danger was in 1941, if I remember. There were rumors there was a-- there was a camp. It was called Majdanek, you know? Majdanek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never heard about it, but that was the first camp, concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Majdanek? M-A-J- D-- D-

speaker: interviewer
text: D.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] E-N-K. Majdanek. I mean, everybody knew about it afterward. That was near Lublin, Lublin. Lublin is L-- L-U-B-L-I-N, Lublin, in the surrounding of there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And rumors came that they-- they take-- take a lot of Jews, they put 'em in trucks, they gas them, you know? That wasn't called crematorium, but that's the way the-- that's-- that's the method they-- they applied. And we knew about it. People came and they told us. But, we said, eh, not going to happen to us, you know? So that was in 1941. But It didn't take long. A year later, we heard other rumors and all that. So everybody was talking. And they said, if you have a place where to work, the youngsters, you'll survive. You'll survive the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my father, he created like places where to work and-- and put people on the list, you know? So we were on the list. And once you're on the list, so you-- when something happens, we're going to survive, because they need us for work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So what happened then, you know? Where did you work at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time also not far from-- from the hometown. And should we get into the war years now? Or you wanna--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I have to continue what-- [INAUDIBLE] with the time, you know? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So one-- a week before-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] just a week before, a couple of weeks, so we had a-- a relative in a neighborhood town, maybe 10 kilometers away from us, by the name Ostrowiec, it was named. It was a-- a brother of my aunt's came-- they were supposed to surround-- the rumor is they were going to surround the city of Ostrowiec so he wanted to-- so he came to our-- to our home to hide until the-- the thing will be over. And then he'll go back. So he's begging my father should give him shoes, you know, we're going to have to run [INAUDIBLE]. So on this-- this-- his name was Pinchas Applebaum, and he had a brother-in-law by the name [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And they formed also a place for work, maybe five miles away from our town. It was called [INAUDIBLE]. That was all bought off for money, you know, to make a place for work. But then nobody worked. It was just a name and they had a permit from the SS. That was all bought off for money.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how do they get their money? They-- they approach a lot of rich Jews. You want to-- you want to survive? You give me $1,000. It'll get in you in on the list. They had a permit of-- You can finish.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a permit for 500 people in this place. It's called [INAUDIBLE], you know? So-- so this Pinchas, he was a relative, so he came to my father and he asked him for shoes, which did, my father-- he stayed maybe a few days until they took away-- they evacuated the-- the city where he was supposed to go back. And then he went back with his brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he promised the minute-- if something happened to us, we'll send a car. We'll take out the whole family, just for a promise. Well, we believed him. So he went back. And it happened so a week later, they surrounded-- oh, you're finished? About finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop here.
